name: Najdi Arabic
notes: Najdi Arabic (نجدي), central Arabia incl. Riyadh; the prestige variety within Saudi Arabia. Primary reference Ingham (1994) Najdi Arabic: Central Arabian. DEFINING FEATURES modeled here: (1) qaf ق → /ɡ/ (Bedouin/traditional; /q/ retained in MSA loans/proper names, e.g. القرآن); (2) VELAR AFFRICATION — /k/ → [ts] and /ɡ/ → [dz] in the environment of front vowels, declared as allophone_rules NAJD_AFFRIC_* (Ingham 1994; Al Mahmoud 2020). It is FREE VARIATION, not obligatory, and it is triggered by a front vowel in the same syllable — so it is scoped here to the unambiguous front vowels only. /a/ is excluded on purpose: the fatha environment splits lexically (dog [tsɛlb] vs heart [ɡʌlb], Al Mahmoud 2020 §3.1) and the orthography cannot tell the two apart; (3) GAHAWA SYNDROME — epenthetic /a/ after a guttural /h x ɣ ħ ʕ/ in a low-vowel+guttural+consonant context (gahwa → gahawa), allophone_rules NAJD_GAHAWA_* (Ingham 1994); (4) interdentals PRESERVED — ث /θ/, ذ /ð/; the Old-Arabic ض/ظ contrast is neutralised to a single emphatic interdental [ðˤ] (Ingham 1994), so ض is transcribed [ðˤ] (with urban [dˤ] variant); (5) emphatic (pharyngealization) spreading backs low vowels to [ɑ]/[ɑː] — inherited from the ar-x-peninsular parent (AR_PEN_EMPH_*), not restated here. jim ج → /dʒ/ (urban) ~ /ɡ/ (Bedouin). No English-style stress block: Najdi stress is quantity-sensitive and not orthography-predictable. INPUT CONTRACT: like all dialect specs this transcribes what the orthography+harakat give; dialectal Arabic is usually written defectively, so short vowels surface only when the input is vocalised. AFFRICATION IS LEXICALIZED/RECEDING: the velar affrication modeled here is characteristic and diagnostic of traditional Najdi, but Johnstone (via Alshammari 2026) and Al-Rojaie (2013, on the Qaṣīm sub-variety) show it is lexically restricted and undergoing levelling toward plain [k]/[ɡ] among younger, urban and educated speakers — the transcriber emits the affricate as the dialect-diagnostic reflex, and this caveat is honest. DOCUMENTED BUT NOT ENGINE-EXPRESSIBLE: Najdi also has resyllabification by elision of an unstressed high vowel in an open syllable (Ingham 1994; the *katab → ktab / *kitaːb → ktaːb pattern, giving legal initial CC clusters), but o2i transcribes the vowels the orthography writes and does not resyllabify, so this is left as a note. NO ar-SA UMBRELLA NODE: Saudi Arabia's dialects do not form a single phonological group — they split across the Najdi (this node and its children ar-SA-x-qassim Qaṣīm), Hejazi (ar-SA-x-hejaz), Gulf (ar-SA-x-sharqiyya, Eastern Province) and Southwestern (ar-SA-x-rijal-alma, ʿAsīr/Tihāmah) types — so the bare country code ar-SA is deliberately left unassigned (lang-code accuracy: null beats wrong; Ingham 1994; Prochazka's 1988 country-wide survey treats Saudi Arabia as a dialect patchwork).

sources.author: Ingham, B.
sources.title: Najdi Arabic: Central Arabian
sources.notes: Primary reference: /k/→[ts], /ɡ/→[dz] affrication in front-vowel environments (كلب [tsalb]); gahawa-syndrome epenthetic /a/ after gutturals; ض/ظ merge to [ðˤ]; qaf→/ɡ/.

sources.author: Al Mahmoud, M.S.
sources.title: A Constraint-based Analysis of Velar Affrication in Najdi vs. Hijazi Arabic
sources.notes: Native-informant data. Affrication is free variation ([k]~[ts], [ɡ]~[dz]) triggered by a front vowel in the same syllable, blocked by central/back vowels: [tsis] 'bag', [dits] 'rooster', [ʕɪlts] 'gum', [tsef] 'how', [dzɛlb] 'opposite', [rɪzdz] 'provision' vs [kʊm] 'cuff', [ʃok] 'thorn', [ɡʌlb] 'heart', [ɡʌbəl] 'before'. §3.1 shows the fatha environment splits lexically (front [kɛlb] vs central [qʌlb]). Confirms Hijazi has NO velar affrication.

sources.author: Alshammari, K.K.
sources.title: An Optimality-Theoretic Account of Velar Affrication in Northern Najdi Arabic
sources.publisher: Journal of Language Teaching and Research 17(4)
sources.notes: The voiced velar /ɡ/ affricates in a STRICTER environment than /k/ — conditioned by high front /i iː/ — while /k/ tolerates a wider range. Affrication is categorically blocked in back-vowel environments.

sources.author: Watson, J.C.E.
sources.title: The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic

sources.author: Holes, C.
sources.title: Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties

allophone_rules.notes: Najdi velar affrication (Ingham 1994, pp.13-14; Al Mahmoud 2020, Education and Linguistics Research 6(2):62-72, exx. 1-9): /k/ → [ts] before an adjacent FRONT vowel — /kis/ → [tsis] 'bag' (ex. 1). Affrication is FREE VARIATION ([k] ~ [ts]); a single-output transcriber must pick one and picks the affricate, the dialect-diagnostic reflex. SCOPE — /a/ is deliberately NOT a trigger. Al Mahmoud (2020) §3.1 shows the fatha environment splits LEXICALLY: /kɛlb/ 'dog' → [tsɛlb] has a front vowel, /qʌlb/ 'heart' → [ɡʌlb] (*[dzʌlb], rejected by the informant) a central one — and both are written with the same fatha, so no rule reading the orthography can separate them. Reading كلب as [tsalb] would force قلب to *[dzalb] too. Under-applying is the honest failure. NOT EXPRESSIBLE — /aj/ ought to be a trigger as well (/keɪf/ → [tsef] 'how', ex. 4), but adding the diphthong to this context list also makes `aj` an ATOM everywhere, so ⟨َيّ⟩ /ajj/ re-segments as aj+j and the inherited emphatic-backing rules (AR_PEN_EMPH_BACK_A_*) stop seeing an /a/: طَيِّب regresses [tˤɑjjib] → [tˤajjib]. Listing a context must not change segmentation; until it does not, كَيْف stays [kajf].

Najdi velar affrication (Ingham 1994; Al Mahmoud 2020): /k/ → [ts] after an adjacent front vowel — /dik/ → [dits] 'rooster' (ex. 2). See NAJD_AFFRIC_K_BEFORE for why /a/ is not a trigger. KNOWN GAP: the real trigger is a TAUTOSYLLABIC front vowel, not an adjacent one — /ʕɪlk/ → [ʕɪlts] (ex. 3) and /rɪzq/ → [rɪzdz] (ex. 11) affricate a coda velar across an intervening consonant. The rule schema can condition on the IMMEDIATELY neighbouring phoneme and on syllable_position, but it cannot say 'the nucleus of my own syllable is front', so those forms are missed (they come out [ʕilk], [rizɡ]). Under-applying is the honest failure here.

Najdi velar affrication (Ingham 1994; Al Mahmoud 2020, ex. 10 /qɛlb/ → [dzɛlb] 'opposite'): /ɡ/ (chiefly the qaf reflex) → [dz] before an adjacent front vowel. Alshammari (2026, JLTR 17(4)) finds the voiced velar demands a STRICTER environment than /k/ — high front /i iː/ — so this list is the conservative one and, as for /k/, excludes /a/. /aj/ is deliberately NOT listed here although it IS listed on NAJD_AFFRIC_K_BEFORE: Al Mahmoud's [tsef] 'how' (ex. 4) attests the voiceless case only, and Alshammari's stricter /ɡ/ environment argues against extending it by analogy. An attestation of قيد/قيمة with [dz] would justify adding it.

Najdi velar affrication (Ingham 1994; Al Mahmoud 2020, ex. 13 /lasɪq/ → [lasɪdz]): /ɡ/ → [dz] after an adjacent front vowel. Same tautosyllabic gap as NAJD_AFFRIC_K_AFTER: /rɪzq/ → [rɪzdz] (ex. 11) is missed, because the front vowel is not adjacent to the velar.

Gahawa syndrome (Ingham 1994, pp.15-16): epenthetic /a/ is inserted after a guttural /h/ standing in a coda after a low vowel (…aXC…), e.g. gahwa → gahawa, laħm → laħam. Modeled as rewriting the coda guttural to guttural+[a].

Gahawa syndrome (Ingham 1994, pp.15-16): epenthetic /a/ is inserted after a guttural /x/ standing in a coda after a low vowel (…aXC…), e.g. gahwa → gahawa, laħm → laħam. Modeled as rewriting the coda guttural to guttural+[a].

Gahawa syndrome (Ingham 1994, pp.15-16): epenthetic /a/ is inserted after a guttural /ɣ/ standing in a coda after a low vowel (…aXC…), e.g. gahwa → gahawa, laħm → laħam. Modeled as rewriting the coda guttural to guttural+[a].

Gahawa syndrome (Ingham 1994, pp.15-16): epenthetic /a/ is inserted after a guttural /ħ/ standing in a coda after a low vowel (…aXC…), e.g. gahwa → gahawa, laħm → laħam. Modeled as rewriting the coda guttural to guttural+[a].

Gahawa syndrome (Ingham 1994, pp.15-16): epenthetic /a/ is inserted after a guttural /ʕ/ standing in a coda after a low vowel (…aXC…), e.g. gahwa → gahawa, laħm → laħam. Modeled as rewriting the coda guttural to guttural+[a].

location.notes: Glottolog's representative point for Najdi Arabic.

stress.notes: Arabic stress is QUANTITY-SENSITIVE: it falls on a syllable because that syllable is heavy, not because of any orthographic ending, so no ending table can express it. The cascade (Ryding, A Reference Grammar of MSA, CUP 2005, §2.3; Watson, The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic, OUP 2002, ch.3): a superheavy final syllable (CVːC/CVCC) takes the stress (kiˈtaːb); otherwise a heavy penult (CVː/CVC) does (muˈdarris); otherwise the antepenult (ˈmadrasa). max_onset=1 because Arabic onsets are obligatory and hold exactly one consonant — a medial cluster splits, closing the preceding syllable, and that boundary is what makes the penult of mudarris heavy. INPUT CONTRACT: weight is only recoverable from a complete transcription, so this requires diacritized input; an undiacritized skeleton has no vowels to weigh. The registered wikipron gold carries no stress marks, but the benchmark strips stress from both sides by default, so this costs no PER.
